text: After general anesthesia, the patient is placed in a lateral decubitus position.
The surgeon and the camera assistant stands in front of the patient.
The procedure is done totally endoscopically under direct monitor vision.
The incision and parts are then placed.
A 5mm camera part is placed at the 5th intercostal space anterior axillary line.
Two further 10mm working parts are then placed.
One part A is placed at the 7th intercostal space mid-axillary line and another part C is placed at the 7th intercostal space just inferior and posterior to the scapular tip.
A 1.5 cm pod B is placed at the third intercostal space anterior axillary line through which a specimen is removed and subsequent endoscopic sewing is performed.
The 5 mm camera and the respective instruments have been introduced into the chest.
The nodule is localized in the upper lobe using digital palpation.
Then a hook electrocautery is used to mark a circle on the pleural surface corresponding to the location of the nodule with adequate margins.
Once the location is confirmed, the lung parenchyma over the marked area is divided using hook electrocautery.
This is then gradually deepened.
Hemostasis is maintained at all time to ensure proper visualization.
Repeated digital palpation over the marked area ensures that the resection is proceeding in the correct plane and direction.
As the cautery resection proceeds deeper into the lung parenchyma, exposure is enhanced by applying traction proline sutures to the edges of the incised lung parenchyma.
By gradual sequential traction on these sutures, the deeper recesses of the lung parenchyma is clearly visualized.
Once the nodule is clearly visualized, the stage traction suture is placed through the nodule.
By applying gentle and gradual traction on this suture, the hematoma is easily inoculated.
Particular hemostasis of the raw lung parenchyma is then done.
The lung is then re-approximated by endoscopic suturing.
This is done by two-layered closure with continuous proline 4-0 suture.
The first inner deeper layer is approximated with a continuous running suture.
Thank you.
The second more superficial layer is closed similarly with another new running continuous proline 4-0 suture.
Thank you.
Both suture ends are then tied to each other.
This technique ensures good hemostasis without the formation of hematoma as well as airtight closure.
The lungs are then inflated and checked for air leak.
A chest tube is placed and the lung is fully re-expanded and the wounds are closed in the usual manner.
